papers.title: Rattle-and-Break: the Impact of Planetesimal Scattering on Super-Earth Resonant Chains
papers.abstract: The spacings of super-Earths in multi-transiting systems exhibit a distribution that is broad and mostly featureless, with the exception of notable excesses of planet pairs situated a few percent wide of first-order mean motion resonances (MMRs). In this work, we extend the so-called "breaking-the-chains" model to account for both of these characteristics. Assuming that super-Earths are settled into stable chains of resonances after disk-driven migration, we show that scattering a planetesimal population that contains only a few percent of a system's mass can reorganize primordial chains in remarkable ways. The planetesimal scattering "rattles" the chains by repelling adjacent planet pairs wide of their initial MMRs. Some chains remain rattled but otherwise intact and make up the observed excesses wide of MMRs. In other systems, however, this initial rattling sows the seeds of later orbital instabilities that break the chains entirely. If individual planetesimals' masses are of order a Pluto mass or so, the onset of these instabilities can occur tens or hundreds of Myr after birth, naturally explaining the apparent disappearance of near-resonant pairs on this timescale. The origin of such Pluto-mass debris is currently unknown.
papers.summary:
**Why It Matters**

When astronomers first began discovering planets orbiting other stars, they expected to find solar systems much like our own. Instead, they found something fascinating and puzzling: chains of "super-Earths"—planets larger than Earth but smaller than Neptune—packed tightly together around their host stars. These planetary systems look nothing like ours, and understanding how they formed could reveal whether Earth-like worlds are common or rare in the universe. This research tackles a key mystery: why do these alien solar systems have such messy, irregular spacing between planets when they should have formed in neat, orderly chains?

**What They Did**

The researchers used computer simulations to model what happens to planetary systems over millions of years. They started with planets arranged in perfect resonant chains—think of planets locked in a cosmic dance where their orbits are mathematically synchronized, like musicians keeping perfect time. Then they introduced a wild card: leftover chunks of rock and ice called planetesimals, each roughly the size of Pluto, scattered throughout the system. These cosmic debris pieces make up only a few percent of the system's total mass, but the team wanted to see if even this small amount could disrupt the planetary harmony.

**Key Findings**

The simulations revealed a dramatic "rattle-and-break" process. The planetesimals act like cosmic troublemakers, gravitationally nudging the synchronized planets just slightly out of step. Some planetary chains survive this rattling, ending up with the slightly-off spacing astronomers actually observe. But in other cases, this initial disruption plants the seeds of chaos. Over tens or hundreds of millions of years, the rattled planets gradually destabilize each other, completely destroying their original orderly arrangement and creating the messy spacing we see today.

**Looking Forward**

This research provides a compelling explanation for why alien solar systems look so different from ours. It suggests that planetary systems go through dramatic growing pains that can last hundreds of millions of years. Understanding this process helps astronomers predict which types of systems might harbor stable, potentially habitable worlds—crucial knowledge as we search for Earth's cosmic cousins.

tweet_hook.hook: 🪐 Scientists just cracked the mystery of why planets are spaced so weirdly around other stars!
tweet_hook.claim: Super-Earth planets start in perfect orbital harmony, but get scrambled by flying space rocks into the messy arrangements we see today
tweet_hook.evidence: Planet pairs cluster just outside of gravitational 'resonances' - like musical notes that are slightly out of tune
tweet_hook.question: What if our solar system went through this cosmic shake-up too? 🤔

papers.title: Survival of Protoplanetary Disks in Upper Scorpius from Population Synthesis Models with External Photoevaporation
papers.abstract: We present population synthesis models of viscous protoplanetary disks subject to mild external far-ultraviolet (FUV) radiation fields (F UV=1-100\,G₀). Our simulations focus on gas disk evolution, exploring stellar masses drawn from an Initial Mass Function and a range of initial disk conditions. We quantify the fraction of surviving disks across 10\,Myr of evolution, track the evolution of gas disk mass and size, and compare our results with observations of protoplanetary disks in the Upper Scorpius region, including the ten targets studied by the AGE-PRO ALMA Large Program. We find that models combining viscous evolution with external photoevaporation yield disk lifetimes of 3-7\,Myr, consistent with observed dispersal timescales, particularly for 10⁻⁴ ≤ α≤ 10⁻². Low-mass stars (0.1\,M_☉) are more susceptible to disk dispersal due to their weaker gravitational binding, with their fraction among all surviving disks dropping from 76\% at birth to 51\% by 10\,Myr. The majority of the long-lived disks are those with low viscosity α< 10⁻³.⁵ and initial characteristic radius Rc < 125\,AU, while the initial disk-to-star mass ratio does not play an important role. The median gas disk mass and radius of the surviving disks exhibit a sharp decline in the first 0.2\,Myr of evolution, followed by a slight increase that reflects survivorship bias. We also explore correlations between gas disk mass and size vs. stellar mass and FUV strength. Our findings highlight the critical role of external photoevaporation in shaping disk populations even at moderate levels of FUV radiation fields.
papers.summary:
**Why It Matters**

Planet formation is one of the universe's most fundamental processes, yet we're still unraveling how it works. Planets are born within swirling disks of gas and dust that surround young stars—imagine a cosmic pizza of material that gradually clumps together to form worlds like Earth. But these "protoplanetary disks" don't last forever. Understanding what destroys them and when is crucial because it determines whether planets have enough time to form. This research tackles a key mystery: how does radiation from nearby bright stars affect the survival of these planet-forming disks? The answer helps explain why we see the variety of planetary systems we do, both in our galaxy and around stars similar to our Sun.

**What They Did**

The researchers created computer simulations that act like time-lapse movies of disk evolution, following hundreds of virtual protoplanetary disks over 10 million years. They focused on a real star-forming region called Upper Scorpius, where young stars experience mild doses of ultraviolet radiation from their neighbors—not the intense radiation found in densely packed star clusters, but a gentle cosmic sunbath. The simulations tracked how these disks shrink and lose material over time due to two main factors: internal friction (called viscous evolution) that causes material to spiral inward, and external radiation that heats and strips away the outer layers of the disk.

**Key Findings**

The simulations revealed that most planet-forming disks survive only 3-7 million years under these conditions—a relatively short window in cosmic terms. Smaller, lightweight stars are hit hardest: their weaker gravity makes it easier for radiation to strip away their disks. While low-mass stars make up 76% of newborn systems, they represent only 51% of systems that still have disks after 10 million years. The longest-surviving disks tend to be compact (less than 125 times the Earth-Sun distance across) and have low internal friction, allowing them to resist both internal changes and external radiation damage.

**Looking Forward**

These findings provide a cosmic reality check for planet formation theories. They suggest that even moderate levels of stellar radiation significantly impact where and when planets can form, potentially explaining why certain types of planetary systems are rare while others are common. This research will help astronomers better interpret observations of young star systems and refine our understanding of how planetary neighborhoods—including our own solar system—came to be.

tweet_hook.hook: 🌌 Baby planets are fighting for their lives in space - and most are losing the battle!
tweet_hook.claim: New models show that intense starlight from nearby stars is destroying the disks where planets form, explaining why so few survive after 10 million years
tweet_hook.evidence: Scientists tracked how UV radiation strips away the gas and dust that baby planets need to grow
tweet_hook.question: Could this cosmic destruction explain why Earth-like planets are so rare? 🪐

papers.title: Exocometary physics: material release and tails
papers.abstract: Despite decades of observations, the physical processes governing mass loss from small bodies beyond our Solar System remain poorly constrained. These exocomets are often treated as analogs of Solar System comet, yet the stellar environments they inhabit spans a wide range in terms of luminosity, stellar winds, and evolutionary stage, leading to potentially very diverse physical behaviors. Within our Solar System, small bodies lose material through a range of mechanisms, including sublimation, desorption, impacts, and/or sputtering. Once released, the composition and dynamics of the ejecta are then altered by additional processes, such as dust sublimation, ionization, and radiation pressure. In extrasolar systems, these mechanisms unfold under vastly different radiative and plasma conditions, leading to a rich diversity of mass-loss pathways and observable signatures. This work reviews our understanding of the mechanisms driving mass loss from small bodies and the subsequent evolution of ejecta in diverse stellar environments. We compare the physical and chemical mechanisms that drive gas and dust production, and investigate how they scale with stellar luminosity, temperature, and activity. We then examine the processes that modify the composition of the ejecta (e.g., dust sublimation, dissociation, or ionisation) and its dynamics (e.g., radiation pressure or stellar winds). To illustrate how these processes vary across different stellar environments, we use four well-studied planetary systems as case studies: the Sun, β Pictoris, AU Microscopii, and WD 1145+017. By exploring how cometary tails behave under such diverse conditions, this work provides a physical framework for interpreting exocometary activity and sheds light on why A-type stars, such as the famous β Pictoris, are over-represented in the population of exocomet-hosting stars.
papers.summary:
# Cosmic Snowballs in Alien Solar Systems

**Why It Matters**

Imagine throwing a snowball near a campfire versus near a blowtorch—it would behave very differently in each scenario. The same principle applies to comets, those "dirty snowballs" of ice and rock that streak across space with glowing tails. While we've studied comets in our own solar system for centuries, we're now discovering them around other stars, called exocomets. Understanding how these cosmic visitors behave in alien environments helps us piece together how planetary systems form and evolve. It's like having a universal laboratory where we can test the same physics under wildly different conditions—from gentle sun-like stars to scorching hot giants and even dead stellar remnants.

**What They Did**

Rather than making new observations, this research team conducted a comprehensive review of existing knowledge about how comets lose material and form their iconic tails. They compared the physics of comet behavior around four very different types of stars: our Sun, the young hot star β Pictoris, the active red dwarf AU Microscopii, and a white dwarf star designated WD 1145+017. The scientists examined how various stellar environments—with different brightness levels, temperatures, and stellar wind strengths—would affect the same basic processes that create comet tails: ice turning to gas, dust being blown away, and material getting electrically charged.

**Key Findings**

The research revealed that exocomets can behave dramatically differently depending on their stellar neighborhood. Around hot, bright stars like β Pictoris, comets experience radiation hundreds of times more intense than those near our Sun, causing them to shed material much more vigorously and from greater distances. This explains why these hot stars are champions at hosting detectable exocomets—their intense light makes comet tails much more prominent and easier to spot. The team found that the same comet would develop vastly different tail compositions and behaviors depending on whether it orbited a young, energetic star or an older, calmer one.

**Looking Forward**

This framework gives astronomers a roadmap for interpreting future exocomet discoveries and understanding why certain types of stars seem to host more visible comets. As our detection methods improve, we'll be able to test these predictions and gain deeper insights into how planetary systems across the galaxy form and change over time.

tweet_hook.hook: Alien comets are nothing like the ones in our solar system 🌟
tweet_hook.claim: Scientists discovered that comets orbiting other stars behave completely differently than ours due to wildly different stellar environments and radiation levels
tweet_hook.evidence: Different star types create unique conditions - from intense stellar winds to varied luminosity - that change how these cosmic snowballs melt
tweet_hook.question: What would happen if Earth's comets visited an alien star system? 🤔
